messages.content:
In the typical progression of generalized
tetanus, what muscles are affected first?
a. Muscles of the face and jaw
b. Respiratory muscles
c. Laryngeal muscles
d. Cervical muscles
a. Muscles of the face and jaw are typically affected first in the progression of generalized tetanus.

Vaccination reduces the incidence of neonatal
tetanus by an estimated 94%.How do you
schedule immunization of pregnant women as
the primary method of maternal and neonatal
tetanus elimination ?
a. One dose of tetanus toxoid at 4 weeks
AOG
b. Three doses of tetanus toxoid at least 4
weeks apart
c. Two doses of tetanus toxoid at least 4
weeks apart
d. Two doses of tetanus toxoid at least 8
weeks apart
c. Two doses of tetanus toxoid at least 4 weeks apart is the recommended schedule for immunization of pregnant women as the primary method of maternal and neonatal tetanus elimination.

Secondary membranous glomerulonephritis can
be be due to this bacterial infection
a. Filariasis
b. Schistosomiasis
c. Syphilis
d. Hepatitis B
c. Syphilis can cause secondary membranous glomerulonephritis.
Secondary membranous glomerulonephritis can be be due to this bacterial infection
c. Syphilis can cause secondary membranous glomerulonephritis.

Acute kidney injury is defined as:
a. urine output of > 0.5 ml/kg/hour for more
than 6 hours
b. sudden impairment of kidney function
which leads to retention of nitrogenous
and other waste products normally
cleared by the kidneys and is always
fatal.
c. a rise from baseline serum creatinine of
at least 0.3 mg/dl within 2 days
c. A rise from baseline serum creatinine of at least 0.3 mg/dl within 2 days is the definition of acute kidney injury (AKI).

It is the method of choic to determine thyroid
size.
a. nuclear Scan
b. CT-scan
c. ultrasound
d. neck Xray
c. Ultrasound is the method of choice to determine thyroid size.

23-year-old woman presents to your clinic
complaining of symptoms of dry mouth and dry
eyes. She otherwise feels well and reports no
joint or systemic symptoms. On P.E., the only
findings are enlarged salivary glands. Studies for
antibodies to Ro (SS-A) are positive. A salivary
gland biopsy showed lymphocytic infiltration.
Which of the following is the most likely
diagnosis?
a. primary Sjogren syndrome
b. sarcoidosis
c. amyloidosis
d. lymphoma

a. Primary Sjogren syndrome is the most likely diagnosis in this case, as it typically presents with dry eyes, dry mouth, and enlarged salivary glands. Positive antibodies to Ro (SS-A) and lymphocytic infiltration on salivary gland biopsy are also consistent with Sjogren syndrome.

A 65-year-old woman presents to your clinic for
evaluation of new symptoms of burning when
voiding. She has no fever, chills or back
discomfort. Herurinalysis reveals numerous
white cells and bacteria. Which of the following medical comorbidities is most likely to coexist in
this patient?
a. anemia
b. exercise
c. diabetes mellitus
d. analgesic drug use

c. Diabetes mellitus is the medical comorbidity most likely to coexist in a 65-year-old woman presenting with symptoms of burning when voiding and a urinalysis showing numerous white cells and bacteria. Diabetes is a risk factor for urinary tract infections (UTIs) due to its effects on the immune system and promoting bacterial growth in the urinary tract.

Superior vena cava syndrome is the clinical
manifestation of superior vena cava(SVC)
obstruction, with severe reduction in venous
return from the head, neck,and upper
extremities. What accounts for 85% of all cases
with malignant origin?
a. melanoma
b. breast cancer
c. lung cancer
d. hodgkin’s lymphoma
c. Lung cancer accounts for 85% of all cases with a malignant origin of superior vena cava syndrome.

The high rates of pneumococcal colonization
makes adults an important source of
transmission. Pneumococci are intermittent
inhabitants of what organ in the healthy human
a. Nasopharynx
b. Mouth
c. Ears
d. Laryngeal
a. The nasopharynx is the site where pneumococci are intermittent inhabitants in healthy humans.

The high rates of pneumococcal colonization
makes adults an important source of
transmission. Pneumococci are intermittent
inhabitants of what organ in the healthy human
a. Nasopharynx
b. Mouth
c. Ears
d. Laryngeal
a. The nasopharynx is the site where pneumococci are intermittent inhabitants in healthy humans.

this organ are at high risk of developing
overwhelming pneumococcal disease
a. Spleen
b. Gallbladder
c. Appendix
d. Thyroid

a. The spleen is at high risk of developing overwhelming pneumococcal disease. The spleen plays an important role in fighting infection, particularly by removing pneumococcal bacteria from the bloodstream. As a result, individuals who have had their spleen removed or who have certain medical conditions affecting the spleen are at higher risk of developing severe pneumococcal disease.

A thyroid nodule is found on a 40-year-old
woman on routine evaluation. Shehas no prior
history of thyroid disease and clinically feels well
with no symptoms suggestive of
hyperparathyroidism. There is a 20-m
non-tender nodule on the right lobe of the
thyroid with no associated
lymphadenopathy.Thyroid function tests are
normal. Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in the management?
a. repeat assessment in 6 months
b. thyroid scan
c. fine needle aspiration biopsy
d. partial thyroidectomy

c. Fine needle aspiration biopsy is the most appropriate next step in the management of a thyroid nodule found on a 40-year-old woman with normal thyroid function tests. Fine needle aspiration biopsy is a minimally invasive and reliable way to determine if the nodule is benign or malignant. Thyroid scan is not typically used as a first-line diagnostic tool for thyroid nodules and partial thyroidectomy would be premature without confirmation of malignancy. Repeat assessment in 6 months may be appropriate in some cases, but a biopsy is generally recommended for nodules larger than 1 cm.

A 23-year-old female presents to your clinic for
evaluation of red rash over the cheeks with pain
and swelling in both wrists, as well as several
small joints in her hands. She also notes that the
rash gets worse on sun exposure and involves
her cheeks, nose, ears, and chin. Upon
examination it revealed a facial rash with nasal
fold sparing, oral ulcers, and 3+ proteinuria.
Which of the following is the most specific test
for the diagnosis of this condition?
a. lupus erythematosus cells
b. antinuclear antibody
c. anti-Smith antibody
d. anti-Ro antibody

c. Anti-Smith antibody is the most specific test for the diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). However, it is not the most sensitive test and may be negative in some cases of SLE. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) is a more sensitive test for SLE, but it is not specific and can be positive in other autoimmune diseases and even in healthy individuals. Lupus erythematosus cells and anti-Ro antibodies are associated with SLE but not specific for it.

Treatment goals of Diabetes Mellitus:
a. postprandial capillary glucose <200
mg/dL
b. A1C < 7 %
c. BP 140/80
d. preprandial glucose 70-130 mg/dL

b. A1C < 7% is one of the treatment goals for diabetes mellitus. The other goals include maintaining a preprandial glucose level of 70-130 mg/dL, a postprandial glucose level < 180 mg/dL, and a blood pressure < 140/90 mmHg.

On the measurement of dose of dialysis:
a. Body water-indexed clearance x time
product (Kt/V) > 1.8.
b. The efficiency of dialysis can be
measured by urea reduction ratio. The
current target is URR of >55%.
c. The dose of dialysis is governed by
patient size, residual kidney function,
dietary protein intake, the degree of
anabolism/catabolism and the presence
of comorbid conditions

a. Body water-indexed clearance x time product (Kt/V) > 1.8 is the measurement of the dose of dialysis. The Kt/V is a way to measure the amount of clearance of a substance from the body over a given amount of time. It takes into account the clearance rate, volume of distribution, and time it takes for the dialysis to remove the substance from the body. A Kt/V value of > 1.8 is considered adequate for most patients on hemodialysis. Urea reduction ratio is another method to measure the efficacy of dialysis, but it is not used to determine the dose of dialysis. The dose of dialysis is influenced by a variety of factors, including patient size, residual kidney function, dietary protein intake, degree of anabolism/catabolism, and presence of comorbid conditions.

Stimuli that activate signals in the
chemoreceptor trigger zone in the medulla,the
cerebral cortex, and peripherally in the intestinal
tract lead to stimulation of the vomiting center in
the medulla, the motor center responsible for
coordinating the secretory and muscle
contraction activity and leads to which type of
emesis?
a. anticipatory Emesis
b. delayed emesis
c. acute emesis

c. Acute emesis is the type of emesis that is triggered by stimuli that activate signals in the chemoreceptor trigger zone in the medulla, cerebral cortex, and peripherally in the intestinal tract, leading to stimulation of the vomiting center in the medulla. These stimuli can include toxins, drugs, motion sickness, or other causes. Delayed emesis can occur hours or days after exposure to certain chemotherapy drugs and anticipatory emesis is a learned response to previous experiences with emesis, often in the context of chemotherapy treatment.

A diagnosed case of systemic lupus
erythematosus for 10 years now maintained on
hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate, azathioprine
and calcium+vit D. Few Months ago, she noted
blurring of both eyes that gradually worsened.
Presently, she is almost completely blind. Which
of the following medications could have caused
the blindness?*
a. hydroxychloroquine
b. azathioprine
c. calcium + vit d
d. methotrexate

a. Hydroxychloroquine is a medication that can cause ocular toxicity and may lead to blindness. Patients taking hydroxychloroquine are usually monitored with annual eye exams to prevent this complication. Methotrexate and azathioprine have been associated with ocular side effects, but they are less common than with hydroxychloroquine. Calcium and vitamin D are not known to cause blindness.

Identify the correct statement regarding anemia
of CKD:
a. There is a decrease in intestinal iron
absorption in CKD.
b. A normocytic, hypochromic anemia is
observed as early as stage 3 CKD. - no
c. RBC survival is still normal in uremia.
d. The primary cause of anemia is chronic
inflammation

a. There is a decrease in intestinal iron absorption in CKD. In chronic kidney disease (CKD), there is a decrease in erythropoietin production by the kidneys, which leads to decreased red blood cell (RBC) production. In addition, there is a decrease in intestinal iron absorption, which can contribute to iron deficiency anemia. RBC survival may be decreased in uremia, and chronic inflammation can contribute to anemia in CKD, but they are not the primary causes. Normocytic, hypochromic anemia is typically observed in later stages of CKD, not in stage 3.

On a pathologic exam, the presence of
mesangial hypercellularity with expansion of the
mesangial matrix is what classification for lupus
nephritis.
a. Class IV
b. Class I
c. Class II
d. Class III

d. Class III is the classification for lupus nephritis that is characterized by mesangial hypercellularity with expansion of the mesangial matrix. Class I is characterized by minimal mesangial changes, Class II is characterized by mesangial proliferation, Class IV is characterized by diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis, and Class V is characterized by membranous glomerulonephritis.

Which of the following statements is true about
the compensatory response to renal
hypoperfusion?
a. ACE inhibitors and ARBs limit renal
afferent vasoconstriction needed in
maintainingGFR
b. NSAIDs affect compensatory
mechanisms via inhibition of nitric oxide
production
c. Myogenic reflex within the afferent
arteriole leads to constriction in low
perfusion pressure.
d. Tubuloglomerular feedback responds to
increased solute delivery via afferent
arteriolar constriction.

c. The myogenic reflex within the afferent arteriole leads to constriction in response to low perfusion pressure, which is a compensatory response to renal hypoperfusion. ACE inhibitors and ARBs actually inhibit the activity of angiotensin II, which is a vasoconstrictor that can reduce renal perfusion pressure. NSAIDs can also inhibit the production of prostaglandins, which are involved in the regulation of renal blood flow and can contribute to the compensatory response to hypoperfusion. Tubuloglomerular feedback is a mechanism that helps to regulate GFR by constricting the afferent arteriole in response to increased solute delivery.

A 47-year-old man presents to your clinic with
complaints of increased thirst and urination.
Medial history is significant for hypertension
which is well controlled on diltiazem. On P.E.,
the blood pressure is 135/84 mmHg, HR
72/min,BMI >30, and the remaining exam is
normal. Which of the following is the most
appropriate initial diagnostic test?
a. urine osmolality
b. hemoglobin A1C level
c. random glucose
d. fasting blood glucose

d. Fasting blood glucose is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test for diabetes mellitus in a 47-year-old man with symptoms of increased thirst and urination. Hemoglobin A1C level is a test that can be used to monitor glucose control over time, but it is not recommended as a diagnostic test for diabetes. Random glucose and urine osmolality may be helpful as confirmatory tests in some cases, but a fasting blood glucose is the most appropriate initial test.

A 47-year-old man presents to your clinic with complaints of increased thirst and urination. Medial history is significant for hypertension which is well controlled on diltiazem. On P.E., the blood pressure is 135/84 mmHg, HR 72/min,BMI >30, and the remaining exam is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
a. urine osmolality
b. hemoglobin A1C level
c. random glucose
d. fasting blood glucose

d. Fasting blood glucose is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test for diabetes mellitus in a 47-year-old man with symptoms of increased thirst and urination. Hemoglobin A1C level is a test that can be used to monitor glucose control over time, but it is not recommended as a diagnostic test for diabetes. Random glucose and urine osmolality may be helpful as confirmatory tests in some cases, but a fasting blood glucose is the most appropriate initial test.

Development of autoimmune hypothyroidism is
associated with this infection
a. varicella zoster
b. epstein barr virus
c. cytomegalovirus
d. rubella

b. Epstein Barr virus is associated with the development of autoimmune hypothyroidism. Studies have shown that there is an increased risk of autoimmune thyroid disease in individuals who have been infected with EBV. Other viruses, such as varicella zoster, cytomegalovirus, and rubella, have not been strongly linked to the development of autoimmune hypothyroidism.

Skin changes found in hyperthyroidism
a. hyperpigmentation 
b. decreased sweating 
c. hyperkeratosis of stratum corneum
d. pallor with yellow tinge

b. Decreased sweating is a skin change that can be found in hyperthyroidism. Other skin changes that can be seen in hyperthyroidism include thinning of the skin, increased warmth and moisture, and possibly itching or hives. Hyperpigmentation, hyperkeratosis, pallor, and yellow tinge are not commonly associated with hyperthyroidism.

Skin changes found in hyperthyroidism
a. hyperpigmentation 
b. decreased sweating
c. hyperkeratosis of stratum corneum
d. pallor with yellow tinge

b. Decreased sweating is a skin change that can be found in hyperthyroidism. Other skin changes that can be seen in hyperthyroidism include thinning of the skin, increased warmth and moisture, and possibly itching or hives. Hyperpigmentation, hyperker
